items.correct: Ellipses (Kepler's first law), with the Sun at one focus.
items.why: Students think orbits are circular. Most are nearly circular, but Kepler showed they're ELLIPTICAL - this small difference explains why Earth-Sun distance varies through the year.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.
It's Copernicus. “Ptolemy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who discovered universal recession of galaxies?
items.why: Correct answer: Hubble. “Galileo” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Copernicus's heliocentric book published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1543. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
